course_info.title: International Digital Communications
prerequisites: DC201 - Social Media Strategy and Management

learning_objectives: Compare digital media ecosystems across major global markets
Identify cultural dimensions that affect digital communication effectiveness
Map regional platform preferences and their implications for global strategies

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital communication is global by default but effective communication is always local. This course teaches you to develop, adapt, and manage digital communications across cultures, languages, and regulatory environments.
key_concepts: Digital divide: internet penetration, mobile-first vs. desktop-first markets, connection speed disparities
Cultural dimensions: individualism/collectivism, power distance, uncertainty avoidance, and their communication impact
Platform fragmentation: WeChat in China, Line in Japan, KakaoTalk in Korea, VKontakte in Russia
Language and localization: translation vs. transcreation, right-to-left layouts, character set considerations
Regulatory landscape: GDPR (EU), PIPL (China), LGPD (Brazil), and their impact on data-driven communication
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing how the same global brand (McDonald's, Nike, or IKEA) adapts its digital presence across three markets: US, Japan, and Brazil, identifying cultural adaptations in content, design, and platform choice
setup_requirements: VPN for accessing region-specific content (educational use)

review.value: Adapting a message for a new culture while maintaining its intent, style, and emotional impact; different from translation, which focuses on linguistic accuracy but may miss cultural nuance or inadvertently offend

High-context cultures (Japan, China) rely on implicit messages, relationships, and shared understanding; low-context cultures (US, Germany) prefer explicit, direct communication; digital content must adapt accordingly

learning_objectives: Develop content strategies that adapt to cultural preferences while maintaining brand consistency
Identify content types, humor styles, and visual preferences across cultures
Evaluate the centralized vs. localized approach to global content creation

lecture_notes.introduction: What makes content engaging in one culture may be confusing or offensive in another. Humor, visual aesthetics, storytelling conventions, and even color associations vary across cultures. Global content strategy must balance brand consistency with cultural sensitivity.
key_concepts: Content adaptation spectrum: standardized (same everywhere), adapted (core concept localized), and fully localized (created locally)
Cultural content preferences: long-form vs. short-form, formal vs. informal, direct vs. indirect
Visual culture: color symbolism, imagery preferences, typography conventions, and layout expectations
Humor across cultures: sarcasm, wordplay, slapstick, and self-deprecation vary dramatically in acceptance
Global editorial governance: central brand guidelines with local execution authority
lecture_notes.demonstration: Adapting a product launch campaign for three markets (US, Middle East, Southeast Asia): modifying copy tone, visual elements, social platform selection, and content format while maintaining brand identity

review.value: The practice of thinking globally (consistent brand strategy) while acting locally (adapted execution for each market), balancing global efficiency with local relevance
A systematic evaluation of content by local experts before publication to identify potential cultural misinterpretations, offensive imagery, or inappropriate messaging that outsiders might miss

learning_objectives: Develop social media strategies for markets where Western platforms are not dominant
Manage multilingual social media accounts with appropriate tone and cultural sensitivity
Navigate censorship and content restrictions in regulated markets
readings: Quesenberry, Social Media Strategy, Ch. 14 - Global Social Media
China Internet Network Information Center (CNNIC) statistical report summary

lecture_notes.introduction: Facebook and Instagram are not the world's social media. Understanding the platform ecosystems of different markets and the cultural norms of communication on those platforms is essential for any global communications role.
key_concepts: Platform landscape by region: WeChat/Weibo/Douyin (China), Line (Japan/Thailand), KakaoTalk (Korea), VK (Russia)
Multilingual account management: separate accounts per language vs. multilingual single account
Content moderation across cultures: community standards that vary by market norms
Censorship navigation: legal restrictions on content in regulated markets (China, Middle East, Southeast Asia)
Influencer marketing across cultures: KOL (Key Opinion Leader) culture, local influencer norms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a global brand's social media strategy across five markets, evaluating platform selection, content adaptation, and community management approaches for cultural appropriateness
setup_requirements: Multilingual social media management tools (Sprout Social global features)

review.value: The term used in Chinese digital marketing for influencers; KOL culture operates differently from Western influencer marketing, with livestream commerce, platform-specific content formats, and different audience expectations

China's internet censorship system blocks Google, Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, and Twitter; global brands must operate on Chinese platforms (WeChat, Weibo, Douyin) with content that complies with local regulations

learning_objectives: Compare major data privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA, PIPL) and their impact on digital communications
Design consent and data collection processes that comply with multiple jurisdictions
Navigate cross-border data transfer requirements for global communications programs

lecture_notes.introduction: Data privacy regulation varies dramatically by region and is the most significant legal constraint on global digital communications. A campaign that is perfectly legal in the US may violate GDPR in Europe, PIPL in China, or LGPD in Brazil. Understanding these frameworks is essential.
key_concepts: GDPR fundamentals: consent, data minimization, right to erasure, data protection officer requirements
CCPA/CPRA: California's privacy framework and its differences from GDPR
PIPL: China's Personal Information Protection Law and its unique requirements
Consent architecture: designing opt-in flows that satisfy the strictest applicable regulation
Cross-border data transfers: Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules, adequacy decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Auditing a global email marketing program for multi-jurisdictional compliance: reviewing consent mechanisms, data storage practices, and cross-border transfer protocols, then recommending fixes

review.value: Must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous; pre-checked boxes and bundled consent are invalid; consent must be as easy to withdraw as to give; records of consent must be maintained

The GDPR principle requiring organizations to collect only the personal data that is strictly necessary for the stated purpose; collecting 'nice to have' data without clear justification violates this principle

learning_objectives: Structure global communications teams balancing central oversight with local execution
Manage cross-cultural teams with awareness of communication styles and work expectations
Develop workflows and tools for asynchronous collaboration across time zones
readings: Harvard Business Review, 'Managing Across Cultures in a Virtual World'

lecture_notes.introduction: Global communications requires managing not just content across cultures but people across cultures. Understanding how different cultures approach hierarchy, feedback, decision-making, and time management makes the difference between a functional global team and a dysfunctional one.
key_concepts: Centralized vs. federated team structures: center of excellence, hub-and-spoke, fully distributed models
Cross-cultural communication styles: direct vs. indirect feedback, egalitarian vs. hierarchical decision-making
Asynchronous collaboration: tools and workflows for teams spanning 12+ time zones
Global brand governance: maintaining consistency without stifling local creativity
Conflict resolution across cultures: understanding that disagreement is expressed differently across cultures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a global communications team structure for a company entering three new markets: defining roles, reporting lines, approval workflows, and collaboration tools for a team spanning four time zones

review.value: A global team structure where a central hub sets strategy, brand guidelines, and measurement standards, while local spoke teams create market-specific content and manage local channels within the framework

A collaboration principle prioritizing written, documented communication (recorded video updates, shared documents, async comment threads) over real-time meetings, enabling effective work across time zones without requiring anyone to attend meetings at 3 AM

questions.question: Explain the difference between transcreation and translation, and describe a scenario where direct translation of a marketing campaign would fail in the target market.
questions.answer: Translation converts text from one language to another while preserving literal meaning. Transcreation adapts the message for a new culture while preserving intent, emotional impact, and persuasive power, often changing words, imagery, and references entirely. Scenario: A US fast-food brand's tagline 'Finger Lickin' Good' translated literally into Chinese might be interpreted as unappetizing or unhygienic, since public finger-licking is culturally inappropriate. Transcreation would create an entirely new phrase that conveys the same enthusiasm and indulgence in culturally appropriate terms.

rubric.key: Explains the cultural mechanism of failure

questions.question: What does 'glocalization' mean in the context of digital communications, and describe one advantage and one challenge of this approach.
questions.answer: Glocalization means thinking globally (maintaining a consistent brand strategy and identity) while acting locally (adapting execution, content, and channels for each market's cultural context and preferences). Advantage: it maintains brand recognition and strategic coherence across markets while allowing content to resonate with local audiences, achieving both efficiency and relevance. Challenge: it requires significant investment in local expertise, cultural review processes, and governance systems to prevent local teams from straying too far from brand standards while allowing enough freedom for genuine cultural adaptation.

questions.question: Describe two key differences between GDPR consent requirements and US email marketing consent under CAN-SPAM.
questions.answer: Difference 1: GDPR requires explicit opt-in consent before sending marketing emails (opt-in model), while CAN-SPAM allows sending marketing emails to anyone until they opt out (opt-out model). Difference 2: Under GDPR, consent must be 'freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous' with pre-checked boxes being invalid. Under CAN-SPAM, there is no requirement for prior consent at all; the law only requires that commercial emails include an opt-out mechanism and a physical mailing address. This means a campaign compliant with US law could violate GDPR if it includes EU recipients.

rubric.key: Notes the compliance conflict for global campaigns

questions.question: Describe the hub-and-spoke model for global communications teams and explain one scenario where a fully centralized model would be more appropriate.
questions.answer: The hub-and-spoke model places a central team (hub) that defines brand strategy, guidelines, and measurement standards, with local market teams (spokes) that create market-specific content and manage local channels within the central framework. A fully centralized model would be more appropriate when: the brand operates in markets with minimal cultural differences (e.g., B2B SaaS targeting English-speaking developers globally), the budget does not support local teams, and the content is primarily technical or product-focused rather than culturally nuanced. In this scenario, central production with light localization (translation rather than transcreation) is more efficient without significant loss of effectiveness.

rubric.key: Identifies a valid scenario for centralization

major_assignments.title: Global Digital Communications Strategy
major_assignments.description: Develop a digital communications strategy for a brand entering two new international markets. The strategy must address cultural adaptation, platform selection, regulatory compliance, content localization, and team structure.
objectives: Research cultural, digital, and regulatory environments of two target markets
Develop market-specific communication strategies adapted from a global brand framework
Address data privacy compliance for multi-jurisdictional operations
Design a team structure and workflow for managing global communications
deliverables: Market research summary (3-4 pages per market) covering culture, digital landscape, regulations, and competition
Adapted communication strategy for each market with platform, content, and channel plans
Compliance framework addressing data privacy requirements in both markets
Team structure recommendation with roles, reporting, and workflow for global coordination
Sample localized content: one social campaign adapted for each target market

criteria: Cultural research demonstrates genuine depth beyond surface-level stereotypes
Digital landscape analysis identifies platform preferences and usage patterns
Regulatory environment is accurately mapped with specific compliance requirements
Competitive analysis provides strategic context in each market
Strategies are genuinely adapted, not just translated from the home market
Platform selection is justified by market-specific data
Content approach respects cultural communication preferences
Timeline and rollout plan are realistic for market entry
Privacy regulations are correctly identified and addressed
Consent mechanisms meet the most restrictive applicable standard
Cross-border data considerations are addressed
Compliance is integrated into strategy rather than bolted on
Team structure balances central oversight with local expertise
Workflow accounts for time zones and asynchronous collaboration
Sample content demonstrates credible cultural adaptation
Governance framework maintains brand consistency
Rigorous market research demonstrating cultural and digital understanding
Genuinely adapted strategies for each market
Comprehensive compliance framework
Professional team structure and execution plan

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on international communications concepts and compliance

weekly_exercises.description: Weekly cross-cultural analysis, adaptation, and compliance exercises

major_assignment.description: Global digital communications strategy for two international markets

midterm_examination.description: Written examination on cultural dimensions and global platform strategy

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination including global campaign analysis and compliance evaluation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Guest lectures from international communications professionals cannot be rescheduled. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Market research components must be submitted on time for peer review.
course_policies.collaboration: Cultural research may be discussed among peers. All graded deliverables are individual. Sharing strategy documents between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Market research must cite sources. Cultural assessments must be based on credible frameworks and data. Stereotyping without evidence is unacceptable.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need internet access, translation tools, and access to global social media platforms for research. VPN access for educational purposes is provided for accessing region-specific content.
